title: Dangling/unreferenced layers on incomplete pull
body:
**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**

Interrupting a large multi-layer pull, can leave layers behind that are not referenced anywhere. These layers can then not be deleted using any command (aside from `podman system reset`). I think the fact that the layer persists after the interrupted pull is okay, and useful if one later continues the pull. However running `podman image prune` or `podman system prune` should remove these unreferenced layers. Currently only a full system reset remove them.

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. (Optional) Start with an empty podman. (E.g. run `podman system reset`). This makes the issue a bit more obvious.

2. Look at the size of the container storage:
    
    ```
    $ du -hs ~/.local/share/containers/
    28K	/home/cspeich/.local/share/containers/
    ```

4. Pull a larger image and interrupt it, once a layer is unpacked:

    ```
    $ ./bin/podman --log-level debug pull -q mcr.microsoft.com/mssql/server
    <snip>
    DEBU[0035] Start untar layer
    DEBU[0035] Untar time: 0.312338996s
    ^CINFO[0038] Received shutdown signal "interrupt", terminating!  PID=1188955
    INFO[0038] Invoking shutdown handler "libpod"            PID=1188955
   ```

   The image is just one I had at hand. I used the log-level debug and quite mode to better reproduce the issue. Once you see the `Untar time:` part you know that one layer has been extracted (and saved to disk).

 5. Verify that we actually created a layer but no images:
     ```
     $ du -hs ~/.local/share/containers/
     51M	/home/cspeich/.local/share/containers/
     $ podman image list -a
     REPOSITORY  TAG         IMAGE ID    CREATED     SIZE
     ```

  6. Run prune (`podman image prune -af` or `podman system prune -af`).

**Describe the results you received:**

The container storage did not shrink and there is still a layer present.  (`~/.local/share/containers/storage/vfs/dir/b40ed86654e59e1012e1716d5384910f8c3bb58274b7b00fca564a53e` in my case).

**Describe the results you expected:**

The container storage to shrink and the layer to be deleted.

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

This happends indepent of the storage backend (tested vfs & overlayfs) and independet of running as root/non-root.

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
Client:       Podman Engine
Version:      4.3.0-dev
API Version:  4.3.0-dev
Go Version:   go1.18.1
Git Commit:   84c85f4d9457fb35849fa93ba04814df681b9093
Built:        Tue Oct 18 14:36:11 2022
OS/Arch:      linux/amd64
```

comments.body: A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
I think this is still applicable I'm able to reproduce this on this upstream.

The issue is the layer is not part of Podman, as far as podman is concerned this is an external image.

Does the following command fix the issue?

podman image prune  --external

@rhatdan Not sure `prune --external` works for this, but instead I found one issue in `c/storage` as well, the GC code does not correctly works for layers, at least for the layers which were downloaded via `pull` was interrupted in between. I am still looking at it and might open a new issue in `c/storage`. Once fixed maybe  `prune` cause also use `c/storage`'s GC part for cleaning these layers.

This seems to be the same issue I am seeing:

This looks very similar to #3799 which was sadly closed due to inactivity I think?

Some further observations: I had to nuke the whole `~/.local/share/containers` directory structure because the server ran out of disk space. Then after pulling **one** image of https://github.com/zms-publishing/ZMS/pkgs/container/zms (which is about 1,5 gig large) about  27.7 GiB /containers where used in the local storage, most of it looks like many duplicates of the filesystem of that container.  [I've put the file listing into a gist as it's quite large.](https://gist.github.com/dwt/68ea965aa0ccc3cc310f5532dec7693c)

None the less, 25gig storage to storage for one image of ~2 gig size is a bit much.
